validation.comparison_result: Expected: 1230113636.01, Actual: 1230108915.60, Difference: 4720.41 (0.38% error). 1 row differs - revenue value mismatch.

Q6.notes: Query executes quickly (12-19ms range, avg 15ms across runs) but produces incorrect result. Difference of 4720.41 from expected value. All filter predicates verified correct in isolation. Issue may be in data loading, decimal precision, or subtle filter logic bug.

Q6.bottleneck: Correctness issue - wrong result despite fast execution
Q6.root_cause: Result is 1230108915.60 but expected 1230113636.01 (diff: 4720.41, 0.38% error). Manual verification shows filters are correct (shipdate range [1994-01-01, 1995-01-01), discount [5,7], quantity < 2400). Single-threaded verification produces same incorrect result, ruling out race conditions. Possible causes: (1) Ground truth computed with different data/version, (2) Subtle decimal rounding/precision issue in storage format, (3) Unknown filter boundary condition. Filter logic itself is correct when tested independently.

analysis.overall: Q6 fails correctness validation with 0.38% revenue difference from ground truth. Performance is excellent (15ms avg, already faster than DuckDB's 21ms), but incorrect results are critical. The parallel morsel-driven execution with 64 threads works correctly (no race conditions), but there's a systematic error in the computation or data interpretation.

critical_fixes.fix: Investigate decimal storage format and ground truth generation method
critical_fixes.description: The result differs by 0.38% from expected. All filter predicates verified correct manually: date_to_days(1994,1,1)=8766 to date_to_days(1995,1,1)=9131, discount [5,7] (0.05-0.07 stored as int64*100), quantity < 2400 (24.00 stored as int64*100). Single-threaded test produces same wrong result, confirming it's not a race condition. Need to: (1) Verify ground truth was computed on same data version, (2) Check if there's a rounding convention mismatch in decimal storage (currently doing int64 multiply then divide by 10000), (3) Examine edge cases around filter boundaries. Consider using higher precision intermediate calculations or checking if some rows should be included/excluded at boundaries.

performance_optimizations.description: The storage design includes l_shipdate_zonemap.idx (14KB file) but q6.cpp does not load or use it. Zone maps store min/max shipdate per 100K-row block. For the date predicate [1994-01-01, 1995-01-01), zone maps can skip blocks entirely outside this range. At SF10 with 60M rows and 100K blocks = 600 blocks, typical TPC-H date distribution means ~90% of blocks are outside the 1-year window. Implementation: (1) Load l_shipdate_zonemap.idx which likely contains array of {block_id, min_date, max_date, row_count}, (2) Before scanning each morsel, check if morsel's block intersects [8766, 9131), (3) Skip entire morsel if zone range doesn't overlap. Code location: In ScanWorker::operator(), before the for loop at line 112, add zone map check. This requires parsing the zone map file format - check metadata.json or examine the .idx file structure.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-40% speedup (2-6ms saved). If 90% of blocks are skipped, we read only ~6M rows instead of 60M (10x less I/O). HDD seek time is significant, so fewer reads help. Even on warm cache, skipping unnecessary filtering saves CPU. Conservative estimate: 2-3ms improvement (15ms → 12-13ms).

performance_optimizations.description: The filter loop (lines 112-127) processes rows one-by-one with scalar comparisons. Hardware has AVX-512 support (detected: avx512f, avx512dq, avx512bw). SIMD can process 16 int32_t dates or 8 int64_t discounts/quantities per instruction. Implementation approach: (1) Vectorize date filter: Load 16 shipdates with _mm512_loadu_epi32, compare with _mm512_cmpge_epi32_mask and _mm512_cmplt_epi32_mask, combine masks with AND. (2) For passing dates, gather corresponding discount/quantity/price using _mm512_mask_i32gather_epi64. (3) Compare discount [5,7] and quantity < 2400 with masked vector ops. (4) Compute masked product price*discount with _mm512_mask_mullo_epi64. (5) Accumulate into vector register, horizontal sum at end. Handle tail with scalar loop. Alternatively, use compiler hints (#pragma omp simd, __restrict__) for auto-vectorization if manual intrinsics are too complex.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-40% speedup (3-6ms saved). SIMD processes 8-16x data per instruction, but gather operations and mask handling add overhead. Realistic gain: 3-5ms (15ms → 10-12ms). Combined with zone maps: could reach 7-9ms total.

performance_optimizations.description: Current filter order (line 113-121): date → discount → quantity. TPC-H Q6 filter selectivities vary. Optimal order is most-selective-first to minimize subsequent checks. For TPC-H SF10, typical selectivities: date range (1 year / ~7 years = ~14%), discount BETWEEN 0.05-0.07 (2 out of 11 values = ~18%), quantity < 24 (out of 1-50 range = ~48%). Current order is nearly optimal, but quantity is least selective so should stay last. However, if date and discount are close in selectivity, swapping them might help depending on data distribution. Profile to measure actual branch misprediction rate. Alternative: use branchless filtering with masks - compute all predicates, combine masks, use mask to conditionally accumulate. This is especially effective when combined with SIMD (priority 2).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-15% speedup (1-2ms saved). Minimal change if current order is already optimal. Branchless approach reduces pipeline stalls from mispredicts. More likely to yield gains when combined with SIMD. Conservative: 1ms saved (15ms → 14ms).

performance_optimizations.description: Currently each column is stored separately: l_shipdate.bin, l_discount.bin, l_quantity.bin, l_extendedprice.bin. The scan accesses all 4 columns for each morsel. On HDD with 100K-row morsels = 4 separate seeks per morsel (0.4MB date + 0.8MB discount + 0.8MB qty + 0.8MB price = 2.8MB total). Interleaving these 4 hot columns into a single file reduces seeks. Implementation: Create l_q6_bundle.bin with struct{int32 date; int64 disc; int64 qty; int64 price} layout. Mmap once, stride through with 24-byte reads. Trade-off: loses columnar benefits for other queries, so only do this if Q6 is heavily used or create a query-specific materialized view. Alternative: ensure columns are laid out contiguously on disk so OS prefetching works well - check with `filefrag -v` to see if columns are fragmented.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-30% on cold cache (3-5ms saved). On warm cache: negligible. Since current runs show 0.35ms load time (warm), this won't help current workload. Only beneficial for cold-start scenarios. Skip unless optimizing for first-run latency.

performance_optimizations.description: In the tight filter loop (lines 112-127), memory access pattern is sequential but the CPU may not prefetch aggressively enough, especially when filtering is selective (only ~14% of rows pass date filter). Add explicit prefetch hints to bring next morsel's data into cache before processing. Use __builtin_prefetch() to hint at upcoming addresses. Example: At line 110, add prefetch for l_shipdate[start + 64], l_discount[start + 64], etc. Prefetch distance should be tuned based on L3 cache latency (~40-50 cycles on this CPU). For 64-core contention, prefetch may be less effective due to cache coherency traffic.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% speedup (1-2ms saved). Prefetching helps when memory latency dominates. With 44MB L3 cache and 2.8MB working set per morsel, single-morsel fits in cache but 64 concurrent threads = 179MB total exceeds L3. Prefetch can hide some DRAM latency. Conservative: 1ms saved (15ms → 14ms).

summary: Q6 iteration 0 FAILS correctness validation (result 1230108915.60 vs expected 1230113636.01, 0.38% error). This is a CRITICAL issue that must be fixed before performance optimizations. The code executes quickly (15ms avg) with good parallel efficiency on 64 threads, but wrong results make it unusable. Root cause investigation needed: verify ground truth data version, check decimal precision/rounding in storage format, examine filter boundary conditions. Once correctness is established, performance optimizations available: zone map pruning (2-6ms gain), AVX-512 SIMD (3-5ms gain), potential combined speedup to 7-9ms. Current implementation does not use existing l_shipdate_zonemap.idx index, missing significant I/O skip opportunity.
